humanities_role_explanation: Uses narrative text passages from unpublished novels as raw linguistic data to construct a word prediction/cloze task to test long-range discourse understanding, rather than for native literary analysis.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Monolingual English narrative passages consisting of a context (average 4.6 sentences) and a single target sentence with the final word masked.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Predicting the final word of a narrative passage, requiring the model to maintain discourse-level coherence over a broad textual window, track character referents, and resolve pragmatic contexts.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed to evaluate general text understanding and contextual language modeling capabilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated older neural language models (LSTM, RNN, Memory Networks) and statistical N-Gram models active in 2016, rather than modern transformer-based generative LLMs.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative language models are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Uses narrative text from unpublished novels (Book Corpus), mapped to Literature and Literary Theory, and Language and Linguistics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates systems using Accuracy, Perplexity, and Median Rank of the target word.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published as a preprint in June 2016.

english_language.note: The paper and the dataset are entirely in English.

summary: The paper introduces the LAMBADA dataset, a word prediction benchmark consisting of 10,022 narrative passages designed to test a model's ability to utilize broad discourse context. Passages are selected such that human evaluators can easily guess the final word when given the full multi-sentence context, but cannot do so when exposed only to the final target sentence.
key_findings: All evaluated neural and statistical language models (including LSTM, RNN, and Memory Networks) failed to achieve even 1% accuracy on the LAMBADA dataset, despite showing strong performance (up to 21.9% accuracy) on an unfiltered control dataset. A simple baseline of picking a random capitalized word from the passage achieved 7.3% accuracy, significantly outperforming all tested language models.
review_notes: Disagreement on 'humanities_role' (Ciop: content, Cip: both) resolved to 'content' because literary novels are used strictly as a linguistic corpus for testing general language modelling (discourse context tracking) rather than evaluating domain-specific humanities knowledge or literary analysis. Analytical tasks and summaries were merged for completeness.
paper_name: The LAMBADA dataset: Word prediction requiring a broad discourse context
